title: Week 12: What Does BERT ‘Know’ About Mercantilism? Probing Embeddings Across Centuries
text:
In Week 10, we ended with an important insight: spaCy’s NER model works by building contextual token embeddings—numerical representations that capture not just a word’s meaning in isolation, but its meaning in context. The example we used was “Apple”: the word gets a different embedding in “Apple released a new phone” versus “I ate an apple.”
This week, we are going to take that insight further and ask a harder question: what happens when the context is not just a different sentence, but a different century?
We will work with BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers), a model that builds contextual embeddings using a transformer architecture. If you have used spaCy’s transformer-based pipelines (en_core_web_trf), you have already used something similar under the hood. The key difference from spaCy’s default CNN-based embeddings is that BERT uses self-attention: every token’s representation is informed by every other token in the sentence simultaneously, not just a local window.
Our plan is to:

Extract BERT embeddings for key mercantilist terms from our Early Modern texts
Extract BERT embeddings for the same words from modern economics texts
Visualize the embedding space using dimensionality reduction (PCA and t-SNE)
Interpret what we find, and discuss the implications for NER on historical corpora

Why this matters for NER: if BERT was pre-trained on modern internet text (Wikipedia, BooksCorpus), then its contextual representations of words in 17th-century prose may be systematically different from what it “expects.” This has direct consequences for how well NER models built on BERT will perform on historical documents—which is exactly what the Ehrmann et al. survey we discussed warns about.



Load historical texts from the week_12_texts/ folder
Extract sentences containing our target words
Build a small comparison corpus of modern economics sentences
Load a pre-trained BERT model (from Hugging Face)
Extract contextual embeddings for each target word in each sentence
Reduce dimensionality with PCA and t-SNE
Visualize and interpret the embedding space
Discuss what this means for NER on historical documents





VSCode installed and your project folder ready (e.g., IDS_570_TAD/)
A subfolder: week_12_texts/ containing the 49 .txt files I uploaded on Canvas
Your Python virtual environment (.venv) activated
Packages we will use:

transformers → the Hugging Face library for loading BERT
torch → PyTorch, the deep learning backend that BERT runs on
scikit-learn → for PCA and t-SNE
matplotlib → for visualization
nltk → for sentence tokenization (you should already have this from earlier weeks)


(We will install the new ones together below.)



You should already have nltk, scikit-learn, and matplotlib from previous weeks. We need to add transformers and torch:
pip install transformers torch
Note: torch (PyTorch) is a large package. The download may take a few minutes depending on your connection. If you have a GPU and want to use it, see the PyTorch installation page for platform-specific instructions. For this tutorial, CPU is perfectly fine—we are processing individual sentences, not training a model.


Create a file step0_check_setup.py and run it:
import torch
from transformers import BertTokenizer, BertModel
import nltk

print("PyTorch version:", torch.__version__)
print("CUDA available:", torch.cuda.is_available())

# This will download the model the first time (~440 MB)
print("\nLoading BERT tokenizer and model (first run will download)...")
tokenizer = BertTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model = BertModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

print("BERT loaded successfully.")
print("Model hidden size:", model.config.hidden_size)
You should see:

PyTorch version (something like 2.x.x)
CUDA available: True or False (either is fine)
The model downloading on first run (be patient)
Model hidden size: 768

That 768 is important: it tells us that every token in every sentence will be represented as a vector of 768 numbers. This is the embedding space we are going to explore.




We need to pull out sentences from our Early Modern corpus that contain specific target words. These are the words whose contextual embeddings we want to compare across centuries.


Our target words are terms that appear in mercantilist texts and in modern economics, but whose meanings have shifted over time. Here is our list, with brief notes on why each is interesting:

“trade” — core concept in both periods; likely to show some overlap but different connotations (bilateral exchange vs. abstract economic policy)
“balance” — in mercantilist texts, almost always “balance of trade” (a specific doctrine about gold flows); in modern economics, broader usage
“manufacture” — etymologically “making by hand” (Latin manu factus); in Early Modern texts, closer to this artisanal meaning; in modern usage, industrial mass production
“stock” — in mercantilist texts, often means goods, inventory, or even livestock; in modern texts, usually financial shares or capital stock
“interest” — mercantilist usage often means political advantage or national concern; modern usage skews toward financial interest rates
“commerce” — used in both periods, but with different institutional assumptions
“plantation” — in Early Modern texts, often means a colonial settlement or the act of planting/establishing; modern usage is narrower

Create a file step1_extract_historical_sentences.py and run it:
from pathlib import Path
import nltk
import json
import re

nltk.download("punkt", quiet=True)
nltk.download("punkt_tab", quiet=True)

TEXT_DIR = Path("week_12_texts")
txt_paths = sorted(TEXT_DIR.glob("*.txt"))

print(f"Found {len(txt_paths)} text files.")

# Our target words
TARGET_WORDS = ["trade", "balance", "manufacture", "stock", "interest", "commerce", "plantation"]

# We will store results as a list of dictionaries
results = []

for path in txt_paths:
    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8", errors="ignore") as f:
        text = f.read()

    sentences = nltk.sent_tokenize(text)

    for sent in sentences:
        # Clean up whitespace (Early Modern texts can have odd formatting)
        sent_clean = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', sent).strip()

        # Skip very short or very long sentences
        word_count = len(sent_clean.split())
        if word_count < 5 or word_count > 80:
            continue

        sent_lower = sent_clean.lower()

        for target in TARGET_WORDS:
            # Match whole words only
            if re.search(r'\b' + target + r'\b', sent_lower):
                results.append({
                    "sentence": sent_clean,
                    "target_word": target,
                    "source": "historical",
                    "file": path.name
                })

print(f"\nTotal sentence-word pairs extracted: {len(results)}")
print("\nBreakdown by target word:")
for word in TARGET_WORDS:
    count = sum(1 for r in results if r["target_word"] == word)
    print(f"  {word}: {count}")

# Save
Path("data").mkdir(exist_ok=True)
with open(Path("data") / "historical_sentences.json", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    json.dump(results, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)

print("\nSaved to data/historical_sentences.json")
You will likely see that some words (like “trade”) appear in thousands of sentences, while others (like “plantation”) may appear in far fewer. That is fine—we will sample down later. For now, we want to capture everything.



At the bottom of the same file, add:
# Show one example for each target word
print("\n--- Example sentences ---")
for word in TARGET_WORDS:
    example = next((r for r in results if r["target_word"] == word), None)
    if example:
        print(f"\n[{word}] ({example['file']}):")
        print(f"  {example['sentence'][:200]}")
Read through these examples. Do you see Early Modern spellings or usage that looks different from how we would use these words today? This is already a hint at what we will find in the embedding space.




We need modern sentences using the same target words. We will create these by hand—this is intentional. I want you to see exactly what the modern contexts look like so that when we visualize the results, you can interpret them.
Create a file step2_modern_sentences.py and run it:

from pathlib import Path

# These are written to reflect contemporary usage.

# Add source label

target_words = ["trade", "balance", "manufacture", "stock", "interest", "commerce", "plantation"]

    count = sum(1 for s in modern_sentences if s["target_word"] == word)
    print(f"  {word}: {count}")

# Save

Take a moment to read through some of these sentences. Notice how “stock” almost always refers to financial instruments or capital, how “interest” overwhelmingly means interest rates, how “manufacture” implies industrial production. These are the modern default meanings that BERT learned during pre-training.



We likely have far more historical sentences than modern ones for some words. To make a fair comparison, we will sample an equal number from each source for each target word. This is important: otherwise the visualization would be dominated by the more abundant class.
Create a file step3_combine_and_sample.py and run it:
import json
import random
from pathlib import Path

random.seed(42)

DATA_DIR = Path("data")

with open(DATA_DIR / "historical_sentences.json", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    historical = json.load(f)

with open(DATA_DIR / "modern_sentences.json", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    modern = json.load(f)

TARGET_WORDS = ["trade", "balance", "manufacture", "stock", "interest", "commerce", "plantation"]
MAX_PER_GROUP = 15  # At most 15 sentences per word per source

sampled = []

for word in TARGET_WORDS:
    hist_for_word = [r for r in historical if r["target_word"] == word]
    mod_for_word = [r for r in modern if r["target_word"] == word]

    # Sample (or take all if fewer than MAX_PER_GROUP)
    hist_sample = random.sample(hist_for_word, min(MAX_PER_GROUP, len(hist_for_word)))
    mod_sample = random.sample(mod_for_word, min(MAX_PER_GROUP, len(mod_for_word)))

    sampled.extend(hist_sample)
    sampled.extend(mod_sample)

    print(f"{word}: {len(hist_sample)} historical, {len(mod_sample)} modern")

print(f"\nTotal sampled sentences: {len(sampled)}")

with open(DATA_DIR / "sampled_sentences.json", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    json.dump(sampled, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)

print("Saved to data/sampled_sentences.json")
You should see roughly 15 sentences per word per source (some historical words may have fewer if they are rare in the 49 texts). The total should be somewhere around 200 sentences—a manageable size for BERT processing on a CPU.



This is the conceptual core of the tutorial. We are going to take each sentence, pass it through BERT, and extract the embedding vector for our target word in that specific sentence context. This is the same principle from Week 10—contextual embeddings—but now we are using a much more powerful model.


When you pass a sentence through BERT:

The tokenizer breaks the sentence into subword tokens. BERT uses WordPiece tokenization, so a word like “manufacture” might stay as one token, but “merchandizing” could become ["merchant", "##izing"]. This is different from spaCy’s tokenization and you should be aware of it.
The tokens pass through 12 transformer layers (in bert-base). Each layer applies self-attention: every token “looks at” every other token to build up its representation.
The output is a tensor of shape (1, sequence_length, 768)—one 768-dimensional vector for each token in the sentence.
We extract the vector(s) corresponding to our target word. If the word was split into multiple subword tokens, we average their vectors.

The result: a 768-dimensional vector that represents what “stock” means in this particular sentence. A different sentence will give a different vector for the same word.
Create a file step4_extract_embeddings.py. Read the code and the explanations before running it—this step will take a few minutes.
import json
import re
from pathlib import Path

import torch
from transformers import BertTokenizer, BertModel

DATA_DIR = Path("data")

with open(DATA_DIR / "sampled_sentences.json", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    sampled = json.load(f)

print(f"Loaded {len(sampled)} sentence-word pairs.")

# Load BERT
print("Loading BERT model...")
tokenizer = BertTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model = BertModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model.eval()  # Set to evaluation mode (no dropout)

def get_word_embedding(sentence, target_word, tokenizer, model):
    """
    Extract the contextual embedding for a target word in a sentence.

    Steps:
    1. Tokenize the sentence with BERT's tokenizer
    2. Find which token(s) correspond to our target word
    3. Run the sentence through BERT
    4. Extract and average the hidden states for those tokens

    Returns a 768-dimensional vector, or None if the word isn't found.
    """
    # Tokenize
    inputs = tokenizer(sentence, return_tensors="pt", truncation=True, max_length=512)

    # Get token-to-word mapping
    # BERT adds [CLS] at the start and [SEP] at the end
    tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(inputs["input_ids"][0])

    # Find the target word's token positions
    # We need to handle subword tokenization: "manufacture" might be
    # ["man", "##uf", "##act", "##ure"] in BERT's vocabulary
    target_lower = target_word.lower()

    # Strategy: reconstruct words from subword tokens and find the match
    target_indices = []
    current_word = ""
    current_indices = []

    for i, token in enumerate(tokens):
        if token in ["[CLS]", "[SEP]", "[PAD]"]:
            # Check if the word we just finished is our target
            if current_word == target_lower and current_indices:
                target_indices = current_indices
                break
            current_word = ""
            current_indices = []
            continue

        if token.startswith("##"):
            # Continuation of previous word
            current_word += token[2:]
            current_indices.append(i)
        else:
            # Check previous word before starting new one
            if current_word == target_lower and current_indices:
                target_indices = current_indices
                break
            # Start new word
            current_word = token
            current_indices = [i]

    # Check the last word
    if not target_indices and current_word == target_lower and current_indices:
        target_indices = current_indices

    if not target_indices:
        return None

    # Run through BERT
    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**inputs)

    # outputs.last_hidden_state has shape (1, seq_len, 768)
    hidden_states = outputs.last_hidden_state[0]  # Remove batch dimension

    # Average the hidden states for the target word's tokens
    word_embedding = hidden_states[target_indices].mean(dim=0)

    return word_embedding.numpy()


# Extract embeddings for all sentences
print("Extracting embeddings (this may take a few minutes on CPU)...")

embeddings_data = []
skipped = 0

for i, item in enumerate(sampled):
    emb = get_word_embedding(item["sentence"], item["target_word"], tokenizer, model)

    if emb is not None:
        embeddings_data.append({
            "embedding": emb.tolist(),
            "target_word": item["target_word"],
            "source": item["source"],
            "sentence": item["sentence"]
        })
    else:
        skipped += 1

    # Progress indicator
    if (i + 1) % 25 == 0:
        print(f"  Processed {i + 1}/{len(sampled)} sentences...")

print(f"\nDone. Extracted {len(embeddings_data)} embeddings, skipped {skipped}.")

# Save
with open(DATA_DIR / "bert_embeddings.json", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    json.dump(embeddings_data, f)

print("Saved to data/bert_embeddings.json")
A few things to note as you watch this run:

model.eval(): we set BERT to evaluation mode. This turns off dropout (random neuron deactivation), which is only needed during training. Without this, you would get slightly different embeddings each time you run the same sentence.
torch.no_grad(): this tells PyTorch not to track gradients, which saves memory and speeds things up. We are not training anything—just extracting representations.
Subword handling: the get_word_embedding function carefully reconstructs words from BERT’s subword tokens. This is important because if “manufacture” is split into ["man", "##uf", "##act", "##ure"], we need to average across all four token vectors to get a single word-level embedding.
Skipped sentences: some sentences may be skipped if the target word is not found after tokenization (this can happen with very unusual Early Modern spellings). A few skips are normal.





We now have a collection of 768-dimensional vectors. Each one represents a word-in-context. We cannot visualize 768 dimensions directly, so we use dimensionality reduction to project them down to 2D.
We will start with PCA (Principal Component Analysis), which finds the directions of maximum variance in the data and projects onto them. PCA is linear and deterministic—you will get the same result every time.
Create a file step5_visualize_pca.py and run it:
import json
import numpy as np
from pathlib import Path

from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib

matplotlib.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 150

DATA_DIR = Path("data")

with open(DATA_DIR / "bert_embeddings.json", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    data = json.load(f)

print(f"Loaded {len(data)} embeddings.")

# Extract the embedding matrix and metadata
embeddings = np.array([d["embedding"] for d in data])
words = [d["target_word"] for d in data]
sources = [d["source"] for d in data]

print(f"Embedding matrix shape: {embeddings.shape}")

# Fit PCA
pca = PCA(n_components=2)
reduced = pca.fit_transform(embeddings)

print(f"Explained variance: PC1={pca.explained_variance_ratio_[0]:.3f}, PC2={pca.explained_variance_ratio_[1]:.3f}")
print(f"Total explained: {sum(pca.explained_variance_ratio_):.3f}")

# Plot
TARGET_WORDS = ["trade", "balance", "manufacture", "stock", "interest", "commerce", "plantation"]

# Color map: one color per word
colors = plt.cm.tab10(np.linspace(0, 1, len(TARGET_WORDS)))
word_to_color = {w: colors[i] for i, w in enumerate(TARGET_WORDS)}

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 9))

for i in range(len(reduced)):
    color = word_to_color[words[i]]
    marker = "o" if sources[i] == "historical" else "^"  # circle vs triangle
    alpha = 0.6 if sources[i] == "historical" else 0.9
    edgecolor = "black" if sources[i] == "modern" else "none"
    ax.scatter(reduced[i, 0], reduced[i, 1],
               c=[color], marker=marker, s=60,
               alpha=alpha, edgecolors=edgecolor, linewidths=0.5)

# Legend for words
for word in TARGET_WORDS:
    ax.scatter([], [], c=[word_to_color[word]], label=word, s=60)

# Legend for sources
ax.scatter([], [], c="gray", marker="o", label="Historical", s=60)
ax.scatter([], [], c="gray", marker="^", label="Modern", s=60, edgecolors="black", linewidths=0.5)

ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8, ncol=2)
ax.set_xlabel(f"PC1 ({pca.explained_variance_ratio_[0]:.1%} variance)")
ax.set_ylabel(f"PC2 ({pca.explained_variance_ratio_[1]:.1%} variance)")
ax.set_title("BERT Contextual Embeddings: Historical vs. Modern Usage\n(PCA Projection)")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("pca_embeddings.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

print("\nSaved plot to pca_embeddings.png")


What you are looking for:

Circles (○) are historical uses; triangles (△) are modern uses.
Each color is a different target word.
If historical and modern uses of the same word cluster together, it means BERT represents them similarly despite the centuries between them. The word’s meaning (in BERT’s view) has not shifted much.
If they separate, it means BERT is picking up on contextual differences—the surrounding words in historical vs. modern sentences are pushing the embeddings apart.
Words like “manufacture” and “stock” should show noticeable separation. “Trade” may cluster more tightly.

Important caveat: PCA captures the directions of maximum variance, which may or may not align with the historical-vs-modern distinction. If the biggest source of variance is word identity (which it likely is—“trade” embeddings are far from “stock” embeddings), PCA will show that first. The historical/modern differences may be subtler and appear within word clusters.




t-SNE is a nonlinear method that is better at preserving local structure. It often produces more visually interpretable clusters than PCA, but it is stochastic (results vary across runs) and the axes are not directly interpretable.
Create a file step6_visualize_tsne.py and run it:
import json
import numpy as np
from pathlib import Path

from sklearn.manifold import TSNE
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib

matplotlib.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 150

DATA_DIR = Path("data")

with open(DATA_DIR / "bert_embeddings.json", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    data = json.load(f)

embeddings = np.array([d["embedding"] for d in data])
words = [d["target_word"] for d in data]
sources = [d["source"] for d in data]

print(f"Running t-SNE on {len(embeddings)} embeddings...")

# Note: perplexity should be less than the number of samples
# A common rule of thumb is between 5 and 50
perplexity_val = min(30, len(embeddings) - 1)

tsne = TSNE(
    n_components=2,
    perplexity=perplexity_val,
    random_state=42,
    n_iter=1000,
    learning_rate="auto",
    init="pca"
)
reduced = tsne.fit_transform(embeddings)

TARGET_WORDS = ["trade", "balance", "manufacture", "stock", "interest", "commerce", "plantation"]

colors = plt.cm.tab10(np.linspace(0, 1, len(TARGET_WORDS)))
word_to_color = {w: colors[i] for i, w in enumerate(TARGET_WORDS)}

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 9))

for i in range(len(reduced)):
    color = word_to_color[words[i]]
    marker = "o" if sources[i] == "historical" else "^"
    alpha = 0.6 if sources[i] == "historical" else 0.9
    edgecolor = "black" if sources[i] == "modern" else "none"
    ax.scatter(reduced[i, 0], reduced[i, 1],
               c=[color], marker=marker, s=60,
               alpha=alpha, edgecolors=edgecolor, linewidths=0.5)

for word in TARGET_WORDS:
    ax.scatter([], [], c=[word_to_color[word]], label=word, s=60)
ax.scatter([], [], c="gray", marker="o", label="Historical", s=60)
ax.scatter([], [], c="gray", marker="^", label="Modern", s=60, edgecolors="black", linewidths=0.5)

ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8, ncol=2)
ax.set_xlabel("t-SNE 1")
ax.set_ylabel("t-SNE 2")
ax.set_title("BERT Contextual Embeddings: Historical vs. Modern Usage\n(t-SNE Projection)")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("tsne_embeddings.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

print("\nSaved plot to tsne_embeddings.png")


t-SNE tends to produce more distinct clusters. Look for:

Do historical and modern uses of the same word form sub-clusters within the word’s region?
Which words show the most internal separation between historical and modern points?
Are there any historical uses of one word that end up near the cluster of a different word? (For example, historical uses of “interest” meaning “political concern” might drift toward the “commerce” cluster.)

Remember: t-SNE distances between far-apart clusters are not meaningful. Only the local groupings (nearby points) are interpretable.




The global plots give us an overview, but to really see the historical/modern divergence, it helps to look at individual words. Let’s make a plot that shows, for each target word separately, how far apart its historical and modern embeddings are.
Create a file step7_per_word_analysis.py and run it:
import json
import numpy as np
from pathlib import Path
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib

matplotlib.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 150

DATA_DIR = Path("data")

with open(DATA_DIR / "bert_embeddings.json", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    data = json.load(f)

TARGET_WORDS = ["trade", "balance", "manufacture", "stock", "interest", "commerce", "plantation"]

print("Average cosine similarity between historical and modern embeddings:\n")

results = []

for word in TARGET_WORDS:
    hist_embs = [d["embedding"] for d in data if d["target_word"] == word and d["source"] == "historical"]
    mod_embs = [d["embedding"] for d in data if d["target_word"] == word and d["source"] == "modern"]

    if not hist_embs or not mod_embs:
        print(f"  {word}: insufficient data (hist={len(hist_embs)}, mod={len(mod_embs)})")
        continue

    hist_embs = np.array(hist_embs)
    mod_embs = np.array(mod_embs)

    # Average embedding for each group
    hist_centroid = hist_embs.mean(axis=0, keepdims=True)
    mod_centroid = mod_embs.mean(axis=0, keepdims=True)

    # Cosine similarity between centroids
    sim = cosine_similarity(hist_centroid, mod_centroid)[0][0]

    # Also compute within-group similarity for reference
    if len(hist_embs) > 1:
        hist_internal = cosine_similarity(hist_embs).mean()
    else:
        hist_internal = 1.0

    if len(mod_embs) > 1:
        mod_internal = cosine_similarity(mod_embs).mean()
    else:
        mod_internal = 1.0

    results.append({
        "word": word,
        "cross_similarity": sim,
        "hist_internal": hist_internal,
        "mod_internal": mod_internal,
        "n_hist": len(hist_embs),
        "n_mod": len(mod_embs)
    })

    print(f"  {word:15s}  cross={sim:.3f}  hist_internal={hist_internal:.3f}  mod_internal={mod_internal:.3f}  (n={len(hist_embs)}h, {len(mod_embs)}m)")

# Bar chart: cross-group similarity
print("\n--- Interpretation guide ---")
print("Higher cross-similarity → BERT sees historical and modern usage as more similar")
print("Lower cross-similarity → BERT sees a bigger gap between centuries")
print("Compare cross-similarity to internal similarity to see if the gap is meaningful")

words_plot = [r["word"] for r in results]
cross_sims = [r["cross_similarity"] for r in results]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
bars = ax.bar(words_plot, cross_sims, color="steelblue", edgecolor="black", alpha=0.8)

ax.set_ylabel("Cosine Similarity (Historical ↔ Modern Centroid)")
ax.set_xlabel("Target Word")
ax.set_title("How Similarly Does BERT Represent Historical vs. Modern Usage?")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
ax.axhline(y=0.9, color="red", linestyle="--", alpha=0.5, label="High similarity threshold")
ax.legend()

# Add value labels on bars
for bar, val in zip(bars, cross_sims):
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height() + 0.01,
            f"{val:.3f}", ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=9)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("per_word_similarity.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

print("\nSaved plot to per_word_similarity.png")



Words with high cross-similarity (close to 1.0) → BERT treats historical and modern usage as essentially the same. These words have stable meanings across centuries.
Words with lower cross-similarity → BERT detects a meaningful difference. These are the words where historical meaning diverges from modern meaning.
Compare cross-similarity to internal similarity. If historical sentences for “stock” have an internal similarity of 0.85, but cross-similarity with modern is 0.60, then the historical uses are more similar to each other than to the modern uses—a clear signal of semantic shift.





Take a step back and think about what we have found. Write up your answers to the following questions as your deliverable (see below).



Which words showed the most divergence between historical and modern embeddings? Why do you think this is the case? Can you explain the divergence based on what you know about the history of these concepts?
Which words were most stable? Does this surprise you?
What does this mean for NER? If we used a BERT-based NER model (trained on modern data) to extract entities from mercantilist texts, where would you expect it to struggle? Think concretely: if BERT represents “stock” in a mercantilist text as something between modern “inventory” and modern “livestock,” how might that affect entity classification?
Domain adaptation: The Ehrmann et al. survey we discussed mentions that lack of domain-specific training data is one of the biggest challenges for NER on historical documents. Based on what you have seen in this tutorial, why would fine-tuning BERT on historical text (or using a historically pre-trained model) potentially improve NER performance? What specifically would change in the embedding space?
Limitations: What are the limitations of our approach this week? Think about: the size of our comparison corpus, the choice of target words, the fact that we used bert-base-uncased (which lowercases everything—what might we lose?), and the fact that we are only looking at 49 texts from a specific period.





Submit the following:

Your PCA plot (pca_embeddings.png)
Your t-SNE plot (tsne_embeddings.png)
Your per-word similarity chart (per_word_similarity.png)
A written reflection (500–750 words) addressing the five questions in Step 8.

The reflection is the most important part. I want to see that you can connect the technical results to the conceptual issues we have been discussing about applying NLP tools to historical texts.

title: Week 12: What Does BERT ‘Know’ About Mercantilism? Probing Embeddings Across Centuries
text:
Load historical texts from the week_12_texts/ folder
Extract sentences containing our target words
Build a small comparison corpus of modern economics sentences
Load a pre-trained BERT model (from Hugging Face)
Extract contextual embeddings for each target word in each sentence
Reduce dimensionality with PCA and t-SNE
Visualize and interpret the embedding space
Discuss what this means for NER on historical documents

title: Week 12: What Does BERT ‘Know’ About Mercantilism? Probing Embeddings Across Centuries
text:
VSCode installed and your project folder ready (e.g., IDS_570_TAD/)
A subfolder: week_12_texts/ containing the 49 .txt files I uploaded on Canvas
Your Python virtual environment (.venv) activated
Packages we will use:

transformers → the Hugging Face library for loading BERT
torch → PyTorch, the deep learning backend that BERT runs on
scikit-learn → for PCA and t-SNE
matplotlib → for visualization
nltk → for sentence tokenization (you should already have this from earlier weeks)


(We will install the new ones together below.)

title: Week 12: What Does BERT ‘Know’ About Mercantilism? Probing Embeddings Across Centuries
text:
You should already have nltk, scikit-learn, and matplotlib from previous weeks. We need to add transformers and torch:
pip install transformers torch
Note: torch (PyTorch) is a large package. The download may take a few minutes depending on your connection. If you have a GPU and want to use it, see the PyTorch installation page for platform-specific instructions. For this tutorial, CPU is perfectly fine—we are processing individual sentences, not training a model.


Create a file step0_check_setup.py and run it:
import torch
from transformers import BertTokenizer, BertModel
import nltk

print("PyTorch version:", torch.__version__)
print("CUDA available:", torch.cuda.is_available())

# This will download the model the first time (~440 MB)
print("\nLoading BERT tokenizer and model (first run will download)...")
tokenizer = BertTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model = BertModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

print("BERT loaded successfully.")
print("Model hidden size:", model.config.hidden_size)
You should see:

PyTorch version (something like 2.x.x)
CUDA available: True or False (either is fine)
The model downloading on first run (be patient)
Model hidden size: 768

That 768 is important: it tells us that every token in every sentence will be represented as a vector of 768 numbers. This is the embedding space we are going to explore.

title: Week 12: What Does BERT ‘Know’ About Mercantilism? Probing Embeddings Across Centuries
text:
We need to pull out sentences from our Early Modern corpus that contain specific target words. These are the words whose contextual embeddings we want to compare across centuries.


Our target words are terms that appear in mercantilist texts and in modern economics, but whose meanings have shifted over time. Here is our list, with brief notes on why each is interesting:

“trade” — core concept in both periods; likely to show some overlap but different connotations (bilateral exchange vs. abstract economic policy)
“balance” — in mercantilist texts, almost always “balance of trade” (a specific doctrine about gold flows); in modern economics, broader usage
“manufacture” — etymologically “making by hand” (Latin manu factus); in Early Modern texts, closer to this artisanal meaning; in modern usage, industrial mass production
“stock” — in mercantilist texts, often means goods, inventory, or even livestock; in modern texts, usually financial shares or capital stock
“interest” — mercantilist usage often means political advantage or national concern; modern usage skews toward financial interest rates
“commerce” — used in both periods, but with different institutional assumptions
“plantation” — in Early Modern texts, often means a colonial settlement or the act of planting/establishing; modern usage is narrower

Create a file step1_extract_historical_sentences.py and run it:
from pathlib import Path
import nltk
import json
import re

nltk.download("punkt", quiet=True)
nltk.download("punkt_tab", quiet=True)

TEXT_DIR = Path("week_12_texts")
txt_paths = sorted(TEXT_DIR.glob("*.txt"))

print(f"Found {len(txt_paths)} text files.")

# Our target words
TARGET_WORDS = ["trade", "balance", "manufacture", "stock", "interest", "commerce", "plantation"]

# We will store results as a list of dictionaries
results = []

for path in txt_paths:
    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8", errors="ignore") as f:
        text = f.read()

    sentences = nltk.sent_tokenize(text)

    for sent in sentences:
        # Clean up whitespace (Early Modern texts can have odd formatting)
        sent_clean = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', sent).strip()

        # Skip very short or very long sentences
        word_count = len(sent_clean.split())
        if word_count < 5 or word_count > 80:
            continue

        sent_lower = sent_clean.lower()

        for target in TARGET_WORDS:
            # Match whole words only
            if re.search(r'\b' + target + r'\b', sent_lower):
                results.append({
                    "sentence": sent_clean,
                    "target_word": target,
                    "source": "historical",
                    "file": path.name
                })

print(f"\nTotal sentence-word pairs extracted: {len(results)}")
print("\nBreakdown by target word:")
for word in TARGET_WORDS:
    count = sum(1 for r in results if r["target_word"] == word)
    print(f"  {word}: {count}")

# Save
Path("data").mkdir(exist_ok=True)
with open(Path("data") / "historical_sentences.json", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    json.dump(results, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)

print("\nSaved to data/historical_sentences.json")
You will likely see that some words (like “trade”) appear in thousands of sentences, while others (like “plantation”) may appear in far fewer. That is fine—we will sample down later. For now, we want to capture everything.



At the bottom of the same file, add:
# Show one example for each target word
print("\n--- Example sentences ---")
for word in TARGET_WORDS:
    example = next((r for r in results if r["target_word"] == word), None)
    if example:
        print(f"\n[{word}] ({example['file']}):")
        print(f"  {example['sentence'][:200]}")
Read through these examples. Do you see Early Modern spellings or usage that looks different from how we would use these words today? This is already a hint at what we will find in the embedding space.

title: Week 12: What Does BERT ‘Know’ About Mercantilism? Probing Embeddings Across Centuries
text:
We need modern sentences using the same target words. We will create these by hand—this is intentional. I want you to see exactly what the modern contexts look like so that when we visualize the results, you can interpret them.
Create a file step2_modern_sentences.py and run it:
import json
from pathlib import Path

# Modern economics sentences using our target words.
# These are written to reflect contemporary usage.
# You could also pull these from Wikipedia, textbook excerpts, or news articles,
# but hand-crafting them gives us full control over what we are comparing.

modern_sentences = [
    # trade
    {"sentence": "International trade agreements have reduced tariffs on imported goods across the European Union.", "target_word": "trade"},
    {"sentence": "The trade deficit widened as consumer spending on foreign electronics increased.", "target_word": "trade"},
    {"sentence": "Free trade policies have been credited with lifting millions out of poverty in developing nations.", "target_word": "trade"},
    {"sentence": "Bilateral trade between the two countries reached a record high last quarter.", "target_word": "trade"},
    {"sentence": "Trade negotiations broke down over disagreements about agricultural subsidies.", "target_word": "trade"},
    {"sentence": "The World Trade Organization issued a ruling against the proposed import restrictions.", "target_word": "trade"},
    {"sentence": "Economists argue that trade liberalization increases overall welfare despite displacing some workers.", "target_word": "trade"},
    {"sentence": "Global trade volumes contracted sharply during the pandemic lockdowns.", "target_word": "trade"},
    {"sentence": "The trade war between the two largest economies disrupted supply chains worldwide.", "target_word": "trade"},
    {"sentence": "Regional trade blocs have become increasingly important in shaping economic policy.", "target_word": "trade"},
    {"sentence": "Trade in services now accounts for a growing share of cross-border economic activity.", "target_word": "trade"},
    {"sentence": "The government imposed trade sanctions in response to the diplomatic crisis.", "target_word": "trade"},
    {"sentence": "Digital trade in software and data services has grown exponentially over the past decade.", "target_word": "trade"},
    {"sentence": "Economists study trade patterns to understand comparative advantage between nations.", "target_word": "trade"},
    {"sentence": "The trade surplus reflected strong demand for the country's manufactured exports.", "target_word": "trade"},

    # balance
    {"sentence": "The balance of payments deficit led the central bank to intervene in currency markets.", "target_word": "balance"},
    {"sentence": "Fiscal balance requires that government revenues match or exceed expenditures over the business cycle.", "target_word": "balance"},
    {"sentence": "The trade balance improved after the currency depreciation made exports more competitive.", "target_word": "balance"},
    {"sentence": "Achieving a balance between economic growth and environmental sustainability remains a key challenge.", "target_word": "balance"},
    {"sentence": "The current account balance reflects the net flow of goods, services, and investment income.", "target_word": "balance"},
    {"sentence": "Portfolio managers seek to balance risk and return across different asset classes.", "target_word": "balance"},
    {"sentence": "The balance of economic power has shifted toward emerging markets in recent decades.", "target_word": "balance"},
    {"sentence": "Central banks aim to balance inflation control with full employment objectives.", "target_word": "balance"},
    {"sentence": "The company reported a healthy cash balance despite declining quarterly revenues.", "target_word": "balance"},
    {"sentence": "Policymakers must balance short-term stimulus with long-term debt sustainability.", "target_word": "balance"},
    {"sentence": "The balance sheet showed total assets exceeding liabilities by a comfortable margin.", "target_word": "balance"},
    {"sentence": "Economists use balance of trade data to assess a country's international competitiveness.", "target_word": "balance"},
    {"sentence": "The structural budget balance adjusts for the effects of the business cycle on tax revenues.", "target_word": "balance"},
    {"sentence": "The balance of supply and demand determines the equilibrium price in competitive markets.", "target_word": "balance"},
    {"sentence": "A negative balance on the capital account indicates net capital outflows from the country.", "target_word": "balance"},

    # manufacture
    {"sentence": "China has become the world's largest manufacture hub for consumer electronics.", "target_word": "manufacture"},
    {"sentence": "The cost to manufacture semiconductors has fallen dramatically due to advances in lithography.", "target_word": "manufacture"},
    {"sentence": "Automated robots now manufacture automobile components with minimal human oversight.", "target_word": "manufacture"},
    {"sentence": "The company plans to manufacture its new vaccine at three facilities across Europe.", "target_word": "manufacture"},
    {"sentence": "Rising energy costs have made it more expensive to manufacture steel domestically.", "target_word": "manufacture"},
    {"sentence": "Additive manufacture techniques like 3D printing are transforming aerospace production.", "target_word": "manufacture"},
    {"sentence": "The decision to manufacture locally rather than offshore reduced supply chain risks.", "target_word": "manufacture"},
    {"sentence": "Countries that manufacture high-value goods tend to have higher per capita incomes.", "target_word": "manufacture"},
    {"sentence": "The factory can manufacture up to ten thousand units per day at full capacity.", "target_word": "manufacture"},
    {"sentence": "Environmental regulations have increased the cost to manufacture chemicals in the European Union.", "target_word": "manufacture"},
    {"sentence": "Contract firms manufacture generic pharmaceuticals for distribution in developing countries.", "target_word": "manufacture"},
    {"sentence": "The shift from agriculture to manufacture drove urbanization in nineteenth-century England.", "target_word": "manufacture"},
    {"sentence": "New policies incentivize companies to manufacture renewable energy equipment domestically.", "target_word": "manufacture"},
    {"sentence": "It takes approximately six weeks to manufacture a commercial aircraft engine.", "target_word": "manufacture"},
    {"sentence": "The government offered tax breaks to firms that manufacture goods in designated enterprise zones.", "target_word": "manufacture"},

    # stock
    {"sentence": "The stock market reached an all-time high driven by strong corporate earnings.", "target_word": "stock"},
    {"sentence": "Investors diversify their portfolios by holding stock in companies across different sectors.", "target_word": "stock"},
    {"sentence": "The company issued new stock to raise capital for its expansion into Asian markets.", "target_word": "stock"},
    {"sentence": "Stock prices fell sharply after the central bank announced an unexpected rate hike.", "target_word": "stock"},
    {"sentence": "Preferred stock holders receive dividends before common shareholders in the distribution hierarchy.", "target_word": "stock"},
    {"sentence": "The stock of available housing has declined in major metropolitan areas.", "target_word": "stock"},
    {"sentence": "Capital stock accumulation is a key driver of long-run economic growth in neoclassical models.", "target_word": "stock"},
    {"sentence": "The retailer struggled to maintain adequate stock levels during the holiday shopping season.", "target_word": "stock"},
    {"sentence": "Stock options are a common form of executive compensation in technology companies.", "target_word": "stock"},
    {"sentence": "The central bank monitors the stock of money in circulation to guide monetary policy.", "target_word": "stock"},
    {"sentence": "A stock split doubles the number of shares while halving the price per share.", "target_word": "stock"},
    {"sentence": "Foreign investors purchased a significant amount of government stock in the latest bond auction.", "target_word": "stock"},
    {"sentence": "The knowledge stock of an economy grows through investment in education and research.", "target_word": "stock"},
    {"sentence": "Stock buyback programs have been criticized for enriching shareholders at the expense of workers.", "target_word": "stock"},
    {"sentence": "The stock exchange suspended trading after a sudden flash crash triggered circuit breakers.", "target_word": "stock"},

    # interest
    {"sentence": "The Federal Reserve raised interest rates to combat persistent inflation.", "target_word": "interest"},
    {"sentence": "Low interest rates encouraged households to take on mortgage debt for home purchases.", "target_word": "interest"},
    {"sentence": "The real interest rate equals the nominal rate minus the expected inflation rate.", "target_word": "interest"},
    {"sentence": "Corporate bond interest payments are tax deductible for the issuing company.", "target_word": "interest"},
    {"sentence": "Negative interest rates were implemented by several European central banks after the financial crisis.", "target_word": "interest"},
    {"sentence": "The interest rate differential between countries drives capital flows in the foreign exchange market.", "target_word": "interest"},
    {"sentence": "Student loan interest continues to accrue during the grace period after graduation.", "target_word": "interest"},
    {"sentence": "The compound interest formula shows how investments grow exponentially over long time horizons.", "target_word": "interest"},
    {"sentence": "Special interest groups lobbied Congress to maintain subsidies for the agricultural sector.", "target_word": "interest"},
    {"sentence": "The public interest requires that regulators prevent monopolistic behavior in essential industries.", "target_word": "interest"},
    {"sentence": "Banks earn revenue primarily from the interest charged on loans to borrowers.", "target_word": "interest"},
    {"sentence": "The central bank sets the benchmark interest rate to influence borrowing and spending.", "target_word": "interest"},
    {"sentence": "Zero interest rate policy was a controversial tool used during the prolonged economic recovery.", "target_word": "interest"},
    {"sentence": "Interest rate swaps allow companies to exchange fixed payments for floating rate obligations.", "target_word": "interest"},
    {"sentence": "The national interest demands a careful balance between free trade and strategic industry protection.", "target_word": "interest"},

    # commerce
    {"sentence": "Electronic commerce has transformed retail by enabling consumers to shop from anywhere in the world.", "target_word": "commerce"},
    {"sentence": "The Department of Commerce released new data showing a slowdown in business investment.", "target_word": "commerce"},
    {"sentence": "Interstate commerce regulations were established to prevent trade barriers between states.", "target_word": "commerce"},
    {"sentence": "The Chamber of Commerce advocated for lower corporate tax rates to stimulate growth.", "target_word": "commerce"},
    {"sentence": "Digital commerce platforms have disrupted traditional brick-and-mortar retail business models.", "target_word": "commerce"},
    {"sentence": "International commerce depends on reliable shipping routes and port infrastructure.", "target_word": "commerce"},
    {"sentence": "The commerce clause of the Constitution grants Congress power to regulate trade between states.", "target_word": "commerce"},
    {"sentence": "Mobile commerce now accounts for over half of all online retail transactions.", "target_word": "commerce"},
    {"sentence": "Commerce between developing nations has grown faster than trade with wealthy countries.", "target_word": "commerce"},
    {"sentence": "The regulation of commerce in financial services aims to protect consumers from predatory practices.", "target_word": "commerce"},
    {"sentence": "Cross-border commerce requires compliance with customs regulations and trade documentation.", "target_word": "commerce"},
    {"sentence": "The growth of commerce in the service sector has outpaced manufacturing in advanced economies.", "target_word": "commerce"},
    {"sentence": "Social commerce integrates shopping experiences directly into social media platforms.", "target_word": "commerce"},
    {"sentence": "Commerce policy is increasingly shaped by concerns about data privacy and digital sovereignty.", "target_word": "commerce"},
    {"sentence": "The expansion of commerce in the Pacific Rim has created new geopolitical dynamics.", "target_word": "commerce"},

    # plantation
    {"sentence": "The plantation economy of the antebellum South relied heavily on enslaved labor for cotton production.", "target_word": "plantation"},
    {"sentence": "Large-scale plantation agriculture continues to drive deforestation in tropical regions.", "target_word": "plantation"},
    {"sentence": "The sugar plantation system in the Caribbean shaped colonial trade patterns for centuries.", "target_word": "plantation"},
    {"sentence": "Coffee plantation workers organized for better wages and working conditions.", "target_word": "plantation"},
    {"sentence": "Palm oil plantation expansion has become a major environmental concern in Southeast Asia.", "target_word": "plantation"},
    {"sentence": "Rubber plantation output declined after a fungal blight spread through the region.", "target_word": "plantation"},
    {"sentence": "The plantation model of agriculture concentrates land ownership among a small elite.", "target_word": "plantation"},
    {"sentence": "Historians have documented the brutal conditions on plantation estates across the Americas.", "target_word": "plantation"},
    {"sentence": "Tea plantation laborers in South Asia face persistent issues of low pay and poor housing.", "target_word": "plantation"},
    {"sentence": "The transition from plantation to smallholder farming improved food security in several African nations.", "target_word": "plantation"},
    {"sentence": "Banana plantation companies wielded enormous political influence in Central American countries.", "target_word": "plantation"},
    {"sentence": "Sustainable plantation management certifications aim to reduce the environmental impact of monoculture farming.", "target_word": "plantation"},
    {"sentence": "The colonial plantation system created economic structures that persist in many developing countries today.", "target_word": "plantation"},
    {"sentence": "Reforestation through timber plantation projects has been proposed as a carbon offset strategy.", "target_word": "plantation"},
    {"sentence": "The economic legacy of plantation slavery continues to shape wealth inequality in the United States.", "target_word": "plantation"},
]

# Add source label
for item in modern_sentences:
    item["source"] = "modern"
    item["file"] = "modern_economics_corpus"

print(f"Modern sentences created: {len(modern_sentences)}")
print("\nBreakdown by target word:")
target_words = ["trade", "balance", "manufacture", "stock", "interest", "commerce", "plantation"]
for word in target_words:
    count = sum(1 for s in modern_sentences if s["target_word"] == word)
    print(f"  {word}: {count}")

# Save
Path("data").mkdir(exist_ok=True)
with open(Path("data") / "modern_sentences.json", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    json.dump(modern_sentences, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)

print("\nSaved to data/modern_sentences.json")
Take a moment to read through some of these sentences. Notice how “stock” almost always refers to financial instruments or capital, how “interest” overwhelmingly means interest rates, how “manufacture” implies industrial production. These are the modern default meanings that BERT learned during pre-training.

title: Week 12: What Does BERT ‘Know’ About Mercantilism? Probing Embeddings Across Centuries
text:
We likely have far more historical sentences than modern ones for some words. To make a fair comparison, we will sample an equal number from each source for each target word. This is important: otherwise the visualization would be dominated by the more abundant class.
Create a file step3_combine_and_sample.py and run it:
import json
import random
from pathlib import Path

random.seed(42)

DATA_DIR = Path("data")

with open(DATA_DIR / "historical_sentences.json", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    historical = json.load(f)

with open(DATA_DIR / "modern_sentences.json", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    modern = json.load(f)

TARGET_WORDS = ["trade", "balance", "manufacture", "stock", "interest", "commerce", "plantation"]
MAX_PER_GROUP = 15  # At most 15 sentences per word per source

sampled = []

for word in TARGET_WORDS:
    hist_for_word = [r for r in historical if r["target_word"] == word]
    mod_for_word = [r for r in modern if r["target_word"] == word]

    # Sample (or take all if fewer than MAX_PER_GROUP)
    hist_sample = random.sample(hist_for_word, min(MAX_PER_GROUP, len(hist_for_word)))
    mod_sample = random.sample(mod_for_word, min(MAX_PER_GROUP, len(mod_for_word)))

    sampled.extend(hist_sample)
    sampled.extend(mod_sample)

    print(f"{word}: {len(hist_sample)} historical, {len(mod_sample)} modern")

print(f"\nTotal sampled sentences: {len(sampled)}")

with open(DATA_DIR / "sampled_sentences.json", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    json.dump(sampled, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)

print("Saved to data/sampled_sentences.json")
You should see roughly 15 sentences per word per source (some historical words may have fewer if they are rare in the 49 texts). The total should be somewhere around 200 sentences—a manageable size for BERT processing on a CPU.

title: Week 12: What Does BERT ‘Know’ About Mercantilism? Probing Embeddings Across Centuries
text:
This is the conceptual core of the tutorial. We are going to take each sentence, pass it through BERT, and extract the embedding vector for our target word in that specific sentence context. This is the same principle from Week 10—contextual embeddings—but now we are using a much more powerful model.


When you pass a sentence through BERT:

The tokenizer breaks the sentence into subword tokens. BERT uses WordPiece tokenization, so a word like “manufacture” might stay as one token, but “merchandizing” could become ["merchant", "##izing"]. This is different from spaCy’s tokenization and you should be aware of it.
The tokens pass through 12 transformer layers (in bert-base). Each layer applies self-attention: every token “looks at” every other token to build up its representation.
The output is a tensor of shape (1, sequence_length, 768)—one 768-dimensional vector for each token in the sentence.
We extract the vector(s) corresponding to our target word. If the word was split into multiple subword tokens, we average their vectors.

The result: a 768-dimensional vector that represents what “stock” means in this particular sentence. A different sentence will give a different vector for the same word.
Create a file step4_extract_embeddings.py. Read the code and the explanations before running it—this step will take a few minutes.
import json
import re
from pathlib import Path

import torch
from transformers import BertTokenizer, BertModel

DATA_DIR = Path("data")

with open(DATA_DIR / "sampled_sentences.json", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    sampled = json.load(f)

print(f"Loaded {len(sampled)} sentence-word pairs.")

# Load BERT
print("Loading BERT model...")
tokenizer = BertTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model = BertModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model.eval()  # Set to evaluation mode (no dropout)

def get_word_embedding(sentence, target_word, tokenizer, model):
    """
    Extract the contextual embedding for a target word in a sentence.

    Steps:
    1. Tokenize the sentence with BERT's tokenizer
    2. Find which token(s) correspond to our target word
    3. Run the sentence through BERT
    4. Extract and average the hidden states for those tokens

    Returns a 768-dimensional vector, or None if the word isn't found.
    """
    # Tokenize
    inputs = tokenizer(sentence, return_tensors="pt", truncation=True, max_length=512)

    # Get token-to-word mapping
    # BERT adds [CLS] at the start and [SEP] at the end
    tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(inputs["input_ids"][0])

    # Find the target word's token positions
    # We need to handle subword tokenization: "manufacture" might be
    # ["man", "##uf", "##act", "##ure"] in BERT's vocabulary
    target_lower = target_word.lower()

    # Strategy: reconstruct words from subword tokens and find the match
    target_indices = []
    current_word = ""
    current_indices = []

    for i, token in enumerate(tokens):
        if token in ["[CLS]", "[SEP]", "[PAD]"]:
            # Check if the word we just finished is our target
            if current_word == target_lower and current_indices:
                target_indices = current_indices
                break
            current_word = ""
            current_indices = []
            continue

        if token.startswith("##"):
            # Continuation of previous word
            current_word += token[2:]
            current_indices.append(i)
        else:
            # Check previous word before starting new one
            if current_word == target_lower and current_indices:
                target_indices = current_indices
                break
            # Start new word
            current_word = token
            current_indices = [i]

    # Check the last word
    if not target_indices and current_word == target_lower and current_indices:
        target_indices = current_indices

    if not target_indices:
        return None

    # Run through BERT
    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = model(**inputs)

    # outputs.last_hidden_state has shape (1, seq_len, 768)
    hidden_states = outputs.last_hidden_state[0]  # Remove batch dimension

    # Average the hidden states for the target word's tokens
    word_embedding = hidden_states[target_indices].mean(dim=0)

    return word_embedding.numpy()


# Extract embeddings for all sentences
print("Extracting embeddings (this may take a few minutes on CPU)...")

embeddings_data = []
skipped = 0

for i, item in enumerate(sampled):
    emb = get_word_embedding(item["sentence"], item["target_word"], tokenizer, model)

    if emb is not None:
        embeddings_data.append({
            "embedding": emb.tolist(),
            "target_word": item["target_word"],
            "source": item["source"],
            "sentence": item["sentence"]
        })
    else:
        skipped += 1

    # Progress indicator
    if (i + 1) % 25 == 0:
        print(f"  Processed {i + 1}/{len(sampled)} sentences...")

print(f"\nDone. Extracted {len(embeddings_data)} embeddings, skipped {skipped}.")

# Save
with open(DATA_DIR / "bert_embeddings.json", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    json.dump(embeddings_data, f)

print("Saved to data/bert_embeddings.json")
A few things to note as you watch this run:

model.eval(): we set BERT to evaluation mode. This turns off dropout (random neuron deactivation), which is only needed during training. Without this, you would get slightly different embeddings each time you run the same sentence.
torch.no_grad(): this tells PyTorch not to track gradients, which saves memory and speeds things up. We are not training anything—just extracting representations.
Subword handling: the get_word_embedding function carefully reconstructs words from BERT’s subword tokens. This is important because if “manufacture” is split into ["man", "##uf", "##act", "##ure"], we need to average across all four token vectors to get a single word-level embedding.
Skipped sentences: some sentences may be skipped if the target word is not found after tokenization (this can happen with very unusual Early Modern spellings). A few skips are normal.

title: Week 12: What Does BERT ‘Know’ About Mercantilism? Probing Embeddings Across Centuries
text:
We now have a collection of 768-dimensional vectors. Each one represents a word-in-context. We cannot visualize 768 dimensions directly, so we use dimensionality reduction to project them down to 2D.
We will start with PCA (Principal Component Analysis), which finds the directions of maximum variance in the data and projects onto them. PCA is linear and deterministic—you will get the same result every time.
Create a file step5_visualize_pca.py and run it:
import json
import numpy as np
from pathlib import Path

from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib

matplotlib.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 150

DATA_DIR = Path("data")

with open(DATA_DIR / "bert_embeddings.json", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    data = json.load(f)

print(f"Loaded {len(data)} embeddings.")

# Extract the embedding matrix and metadata
embeddings = np.array([d["embedding"] for d in data])
words = [d["target_word"] for d in data]
sources = [d["source"] for d in data]

print(f"Embedding matrix shape: {embeddings.shape}")

# Fit PCA
pca = PCA(n_components=2)
reduced = pca.fit_transform(embeddings)

print(f"Explained variance: PC1={pca.explained_variance_ratio_[0]:.3f}, PC2={pca.explained_variance_ratio_[1]:.3f}")
print(f"Total explained: {sum(pca.explained_variance_ratio_):.3f}")

# Plot
TARGET_WORDS = ["trade", "balance", "manufacture", "stock", "interest", "commerce", "plantation"]

# Color map: one color per word
colors = plt.cm.tab10(np.linspace(0, 1, len(TARGET_WORDS)))
word_to_color = {w: colors[i] for i, w in enumerate(TARGET_WORDS)}

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 9))

for i in range(len(reduced)):
    color = word_to_color[words[i]]
    marker = "o" if sources[i] == "historical" else "^"  # circle vs triangle
    alpha = 0.6 if sources[i] == "historical" else 0.9
    edgecolor = "black" if sources[i] == "modern" else "none"
    ax.scatter(reduced[i, 0], reduced[i, 1],
               c=[color], marker=marker, s=60,
               alpha=alpha, edgecolors=edgecolor, linewidths=0.5)

# Legend for words
for word in TARGET_WORDS:
    ax.scatter([], [], c=[word_to_color[word]], label=word, s=60)

# Legend for sources
ax.scatter([], [], c="gray", marker="o", label="Historical", s=60)
ax.scatter([], [], c="gray", marker="^", label="Modern", s=60, edgecolors="black", linewidths=0.5)

ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8, ncol=2)
ax.set_xlabel(f"PC1 ({pca.explained_variance_ratio_[0]:.1%} variance)")
ax.set_ylabel(f"PC2 ({pca.explained_variance_ratio_[1]:.1%} variance)")
ax.set_title("BERT Contextual Embeddings: Historical vs. Modern Usage\n(PCA Projection)")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("pca_embeddings.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

print("\nSaved plot to pca_embeddings.png")


What you are looking for:

Circles (○) are historical uses; triangles (△) are modern uses.
Each color is a different target word.
If historical and modern uses of the same word cluster together, it means BERT represents them similarly despite the centuries between them. The word’s meaning (in BERT’s view) has not shifted much.
If they separate, it means BERT is picking up on contextual differences—the surrounding words in historical vs. modern sentences are pushing the embeddings apart.
Words like “manufacture” and “stock” should show noticeable separation. “Trade” may cluster more tightly.

Important caveat: PCA captures the directions of maximum variance, which may or may not align with the historical-vs-modern distinction. If the biggest source of variance is word identity (which it likely is—“trade” embeddings are far from “stock” embeddings), PCA will show that first. The historical/modern differences may be subtler and appear within word clusters.

title: Week 12: What Does BERT ‘Know’ About Mercantilism? Probing Embeddings Across Centuries
text:
t-SNE is a nonlinear method that is better at preserving local structure. It often produces more visually interpretable clusters than PCA, but it is stochastic (results vary across runs) and the axes are not directly interpretable.
Create a file step6_visualize_tsne.py and run it:
import json
import numpy as np
from pathlib import Path

from sklearn.manifold import TSNE
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib

matplotlib.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 150

DATA_DIR = Path("data")

with open(DATA_DIR / "bert_embeddings.json", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    data = json.load(f)

embeddings = np.array([d["embedding"] for d in data])
words = [d["target_word"] for d in data]
sources = [d["source"] for d in data]

print(f"Running t-SNE on {len(embeddings)} embeddings...")

# Note: perplexity should be less than the number of samples
# A common rule of thumb is between 5 and 50
perplexity_val = min(30, len(embeddings) - 1)

tsne = TSNE(
    n_components=2,
    perplexity=perplexity_val,
    random_state=42,
    n_iter=1000,
    learning_rate="auto",
    init="pca"
)
reduced = tsne.fit_transform(embeddings)

TARGET_WORDS = ["trade", "balance", "manufacture", "stock", "interest", "commerce", "plantation"]

colors = plt.cm.tab10(np.linspace(0, 1, len(TARGET_WORDS)))
word_to_color = {w: colors[i] for i, w in enumerate(TARGET_WORDS)}

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 9))

for i in range(len(reduced)):
    color = word_to_color[words[i]]
    marker = "o" if sources[i] == "historical" else "^"
    alpha = 0.6 if sources[i] == "historical" else 0.9
    edgecolor = "black" if sources[i] == "modern" else "none"
    ax.scatter(reduced[i, 0], reduced[i, 1],
               c=[color], marker=marker, s=60,
               alpha=alpha, edgecolors=edgecolor, linewidths=0.5)

for word in TARGET_WORDS:
    ax.scatter([], [], c=[word_to_color[word]], label=word, s=60)
ax.scatter([], [], c="gray", marker="o", label="Historical", s=60)
ax.scatter([], [], c="gray", marker="^", label="Modern", s=60, edgecolors="black", linewidths=0.5)

ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8, ncol=2)
ax.set_xlabel("t-SNE 1")
ax.set_ylabel("t-SNE 2")
ax.set_title("BERT Contextual Embeddings: Historical vs. Modern Usage\n(t-SNE Projection)")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("tsne_embeddings.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

print("\nSaved plot to tsne_embeddings.png")


t-SNE tends to produce more distinct clusters. Look for:

Do historical and modern uses of the same word form sub-clusters within the word’s region?
Which words show the most internal separation between historical and modern points?
Are there any historical uses of one word that end up near the cluster of a different word? (For example, historical uses of “interest” meaning “political concern” might drift toward the “commerce” cluster.)

Remember: t-SNE distances between far-apart clusters are not meaningful. Only the local groupings (nearby points) are interpretable.

title: Week 12: What Does BERT ‘Know’ About Mercantilism? Probing Embeddings Across Centuries
text:
The global plots give us an overview, but to really see the historical/modern divergence, it helps to look at individual words. Let’s make a plot that shows, for each target word separately, how far apart its historical and modern embeddings are.
Create a file step7_per_word_analysis.py and run it:
import json
import numpy as np
from pathlib import Path
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib

matplotlib.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 150

DATA_DIR = Path("data")

with open(DATA_DIR / "bert_embeddings.json", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    data = json.load(f)

TARGET_WORDS = ["trade", "balance", "manufacture", "stock", "interest", "commerce", "plantation"]

print("Average cosine similarity between historical and modern embeddings:\n")

results = []

for word in TARGET_WORDS:
    hist_embs = [d["embedding"] for d in data if d["target_word"] == word and d["source"] == "historical"]
    mod_embs = [d["embedding"] for d in data if d["target_word"] == word and d["source"] == "modern"]

    if not hist_embs or not mod_embs:
        print(f"  {word}: insufficient data (hist={len(hist_embs)}, mod={len(mod_embs)})")
        continue

    hist_embs = np.array(hist_embs)
    mod_embs = np.array(mod_embs)

    # Average embedding for each group
    hist_centroid = hist_embs.mean(axis=0, keepdims=True)
    mod_centroid = mod_embs.mean(axis=0, keepdims=True)

    # Cosine similarity between centroids
    sim = cosine_similarity(hist_centroid, mod_centroid)[0][0]

    # Also compute within-group similarity for reference
    if len(hist_embs) > 1:
        hist_internal = cosine_similarity(hist_embs).mean()
    else:
        hist_internal = 1.0

    if len(mod_embs) > 1:
        mod_internal = cosine_similarity(mod_embs).mean()
    else:
        mod_internal = 1.0

    results.append({
        "word": word,
        "cross_similarity": sim,
        "hist_internal": hist_internal,
        "mod_internal": mod_internal,
        "n_hist": len(hist_embs),
        "n_mod": len(mod_embs)
    })

    print(f"  {word:15s}  cross={sim:.3f}  hist_internal={hist_internal:.3f}  mod_internal={mod_internal:.3f}  (n={len(hist_embs)}h, {len(mod_embs)}m)")

# Bar chart: cross-group similarity
print("\n--- Interpretation guide ---")
print("Higher cross-similarity → BERT sees historical and modern usage as more similar")
print("Lower cross-similarity → BERT sees a bigger gap between centuries")
print("Compare cross-similarity to internal similarity to see if the gap is meaningful")

words_plot = [r["word"] for r in results]
cross_sims = [r["cross_similarity"] for r in results]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
bars = ax.bar(words_plot, cross_sims, color="steelblue", edgecolor="black", alpha=0.8)

ax.set_ylabel("Cosine Similarity (Historical ↔ Modern Centroid)")
ax.set_xlabel("Target Word")
ax.set_title("How Similarly Does BERT Represent Historical vs. Modern Usage?")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
ax.axhline(y=0.9, color="red", linestyle="--", alpha=0.5, label="High similarity threshold")
ax.legend()

# Add value labels on bars
for bar, val in zip(bars, cross_sims):
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height() + 0.01,
            f"{val:.3f}", ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=9)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("per_word_similarity.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

print("\nSaved plot to per_word_similarity.png")



Words with high cross-similarity (close to 1.0) → BERT treats historical and modern usage as essentially the same. These words have stable meanings across centuries.
Words with lower cross-similarity → BERT detects a meaningful difference. These are the words where historical meaning diverges from modern meaning.
Compare cross-similarity to internal similarity. If historical sentences for “stock” have an internal similarity of 0.85, but cross-similarity with modern is 0.60, then the historical uses are more similar to each other than to the modern uses—a clear signal of semantic shift.

title: Week 12: What Does BERT ‘Know’ About Mercantilism? Probing Embeddings Across Centuries
text:
Take a step back and think about what we have found. Write up your answers to the following questions as your deliverable (see below).



Which words showed the most divergence between historical and modern embeddings? Why do you think this is the case? Can you explain the divergence based on what you know about the history of these concepts?
Which words were most stable? Does this surprise you?
What does this mean for NER? If we used a BERT-based NER model (trained on modern data) to extract entities from mercantilist texts, where would you expect it to struggle? Think concretely: if BERT represents “stock” in a mercantilist text as something between modern “inventory” and modern “livestock,” how might that affect entity classification?
Domain adaptation: The Ehrmann et al. survey we discussed mentions that lack of domain-specific training data is one of the biggest challenges for NER on historical documents. Based on what you have seen in this tutorial, why would fine-tuning BERT on historical text (or using a historically pre-trained model) potentially improve NER performance? What specifically would change in the embedding space?
Limitations: What are the limitations of our approach this week? Think about: the size of our comparison corpus, the choice of target words, the fact that we used bert-base-uncased (which lowercases everything—what might we lose?), and the fact that we are only looking at 49 texts from a specific period.

title: Week 12: What Does BERT ‘Know’ About Mercantilism? Probing Embeddings Across Centuries
text:
Submit the following:

Your PCA plot (pca_embeddings.png)
Your t-SNE plot (tsne_embeddings.png)
Your per-word similarity chart (per_word_similarity.png)
A written reflection (500–750 words) addressing the five questions in Step 8.

The reflection is the most important part. I want to see that you can connect the technical results to the conceptual issues we have been discussing about applying NLP tools to historical texts.

title: Week 2: Teaching BERT Early Modern English — Fine-Tuning on the Geneva Bible
text:
Last week, we saw that BERT produces powerful contextual embeddings — but we also saw its limits. When we gave BERT sentences from the Geneva Bible (1560), its masked-word predictions were sometimes off, and its confidence was lower than for modern English. This is because BERT was pre-trained on Wikipedia and contemporary books: it knows modern English fluently, but Early Modern English is a foreign dialect.
This week, we fix that. We will fine-tune BERT on the Geneva Bible using Masked Language Modeling (MLM) — the same task BERT was originally trained on. The idea is simple: by continuing to train BERT on 16th-century text, we teach it the spelling, vocabulary, and syntax of the period. This process is called domain-adaptive pretraining.
By the end of this tutorial, you will be able to:

Load and prepare a historical corpus for language model fine-tuning
Evaluate BERT’s baseline performance on Early Modern English
Fine-tune BERT using masked language modeling
Measure whether the fine-tuned model actually improved
Save a domain-adapted model for use on other historical texts (next week)

title: Week 2: Teaching BERT Early Modern English — Fine-Tuning on the Geneva Bible
text:
The Problem
BERT’s pre-training corpus was modern. When it encounters “heauen” (heaven), “darkenesse” (darkness), “vnto” (unto), or “saide” (said), these words either:

Get split into unusual subword pieces (e.g., “heauen” → ["he", "##au", "##en"])
Get misinterpreted because the surrounding syntax is unfamiliar

The embeddings BERT produces for Early Modern sentences are therefore noisier and less semantically coherent than those it produces for modern text.

title: Week 2: Teaching BERT Early Modern English — Fine-Tuning on the Geneva Bible
text:
The Solution
We continue BERT’s MLM training on a large body of Early Modern English. The Geneva Bible is ideal for this because:

It is large (~31,000 verses), giving the model plenty of data
It is clean, well-structured text (no OCR errors)
It is saturated with the orthography and syntax of the 1560s
Its vocabulary overlaps heavily with the mercantile documents we will analyze next week

After fine-tuning, BERT will produce better embeddings for any Early Modern English text — not just biblical text. The model learns the language, not just the content.

title: Week 2: Teaching BERT Early Modern English — Fine-Tuning on the Geneva Bible
text:
What MLM Fine-Tuning Actually Does
During fine-tuning, we randomly mask 15% of tokens in each sentence and ask BERT to predict them. When BERT predicts “heauen” where “heauen” was masked, it updates its internal weights to be better at representing that word and its context. Over thousands of such predictions across the whole Geneva Bible, BERT gradually learns:

That “heauen” means heaven
That “saide” functions like “said”
That “the Lorde God” is a common phrase in this register
That “and” often appears in long coordinate structures (a hallmark of biblical prose)

title: Week 2: Teaching BERT Early Modern English — Fine-Tuning on the Geneva Bible
text:
Baseline MLM Predictions
```{python}
from transformers import pipeline

# Load the base (un-fine-tuned) BERT for masked language modeling
fill_mask = pipeline("fill-mask", model="bert-base-uncased", device=-1)

# Select test sentences — we will use the SAME ones after fine-tuning
baseline_tests = [
    ("And God sawe the [MASK] that it was good.", "light"),
    ("In the beginning God created the [MASK] and the earth.", "heauen"),
    ("The Lord God also made the man of the [MASK] of the grounde.", "dust"),
    ("And the serpent saide vnto the [MASK], Ye shall not die.", "woman"),
    ("And God [MASK] them, saying, Bring foorth fruite and multiplie.", "blessed"),
    ("The Lord is my [MASK], I shall not want.", "shepherd"),
    ("Thou shalt not [MASK]: thou shalt not commit adulterie.", "kill"),
    ("And God called the dry land, [MASK], and he called the gathering together of the waters, Seas.", "earth"),
]

print("=== BASELINE MLM Predictions (Before Fine-Tuning) ===\n")
baseline_results = []

for masked_sent, expected in baseline_tests:
    predictions = fill_mask(masked_sent)
    top_tokens = [p["token_str"] for p in predictions[:5]]
    top_scores = [p["score"] for p in predictions[:5]]
    
    hit = expected.lower() in [t.lower() for t in top_tokens]
    baseline_results.append({
        "sentence": masked_sent,
        "expected": expected,
        "top_prediction": top_tokens[0],
        "top_score": top_scores[0],
        "in_top_5": hit,
        "all_predictions": top_tokens
    })
    
    status = "✓" if hit else "✗"
    print(f"{status} Expected: {expected:12s} | Top prediction: {top_tokens[0]:12s} "
          f"(score: {top_scores[0]:.3f})")
    print(f"  Top 5: {top_tokens}")
    print(f"  Sentence: {masked_sent}\n")

baseline_accuracy = sum(1 for r in baseline_results if r["in_top_5"]) / len(baseline_results)
baseline_avg_score = np.mean([r["top_score"] for r in baseline_results])
print(f"Baseline: {baseline_accuracy:.0%} of expected words in top 5")
print(f"Average top-prediction confidence: {baseline_avg_score:.3f}")
```
Note these numbers — we will compare them directly after fine-tuning.

title: Week 2: Teaching BERT Early Modern English — Fine-Tuning on the Geneva Bible
text:
Baseline Embedding Quality
We also want to measure whether BERT produces coherent embeddings for Geneva Bible text. We will compute similarity between theologically related verses and unrelated verses.
```{python}
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity

st_model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

# Pairs of related verses
related_pairs = [
    ("In the beginning God created the heauen and the earth.",
     "Thus the heauens and the earth were finished, and all the host of them."),
    ("And God saide, Let there be light: And there was light.",
     "And God called the light, Day, and the darkenes, he called Night."),
    ("The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want.",
     "He maketh me to rest in greene pasture, and leadeth me by the still waters."),
]

# Pairs of unrelated verses
unrelated_pairs = [
    ("In the beginning God created the heauen and the earth.",
```
